data.title: Sri Lankan shares log ninth straight session of gains
data.description:
Sri Lankan shares closed higher for the ninth successive session on Friday, led by materials and communication services stocks.

* The CSE All-Share index settled 0.36% higher at 21,598.99.

* The index closed at a record high for the sixth straight session, and logged its highest weekly gain since the weeek ended July 25.

* SMB Finance PLC and Industrial Asphalts (Ceylon) PLC were the top percentage gainers on the index, gaining 33.3% and 25%, respectively.

* Trading volume on the index fell to 148.1 million shares from 261.3 million shares in the previous session.

* The equity market's turnover fell to 6.02 billion Sri Lankan rupees ($19.9 million) from 9.30 billion rupees in the previous session, according to exchange data.

* Foreign investors were net sellers, offloading stocks worth 215.1 million rupees, while domestic investors were net buyers, purchasing shares worth 5.96 billion rupees, the data showed.

* For a report on global markets, click

($1 = 302.5000 Sri Lankan rupees)

data.title: Microsoft Has More Upside as AI Expands, Morgan Stanley Says
data.description:
Microsoft Corp. (MSFT, Financials) remains Morgan Stanley's top pick, with analysts saying the company's artificial intelligence strategy is starting to take hold and could fuel more upside.

The firm said Microsoft's early lead in AI tools, including integration across its Office and cloud platforms, is helping set the stage for stronger growth. It pointed to signs that customer adoption is beginning to show up in revenue, and that the stock still has room to climb despite recent gains.

Microsoft has been among the largest beneficiaries of AI enthusiasm in markets this year, with shares outperforming the broader tech sector. Morgan Stanley's call reflects confidence that the company can turn that momentum into sustained earnings growth.

The stock was little changed in premarket trading Friday. Investors will next look to Microsoft's upcoming earnings report for more evidence of how quickly AI is translating into financial results.

data.title: 'For the Sun After Long Nights' Review: Sisters in Iran
data.description:
By Meghan Cox Gurdon

Since the 1979 revolution in Iran that put Shiite clerics into power, the country's internal unrest has featured in American media coverage with such regularity that, to a casual audience, it's become perpetually old news. Members of the Iranian diaspora, as well as policymakers and specialists in Iranian history and politics, are obvious exceptions to this generalization, but those without a stake or special interest can be forgiven for thinking of Iran less as a real place than as a continual source of turmoil. Most of the time, the thunder is remote — civilians protest, the regime cracks down, the cycle repeats — but now and again the crash is so loud that it breaks through even to the indifferent and the blasé.

The Green Revolution of 2009 got the world's attention, for instance, when vast numbers of Iranians surged into the streets to protest the results of what they took to be a rigged election. In a matter of months, the clerical regime crushed that rebellion, as it had earlier ones. But crushing something does not necessarily make it go away; as any student of physics can tell you, compression has the power to produce a yet more forceful reaction.

It has taken the regime longer to suppress the latest uprising. Three years ago this month, members of the morality police in Tehran beat to death a young woman, Mahsa Jîna Amini, after detaining her for transgressing official rules governing female modesty. An image of the bloodied 22-year-old went viral on social media — and sparked fresh rebellion. Women tore off their scarves and hijabs in defiance of the law requiring them to cover their hair in public. Crowds massed, sang and chanted. The security forces responded with pepper spray, paintballs and live ammunition, maiming and killing hundreds of adults and fatally shooting a 9-year-old boy. Thousands were harassed, beaten, arrested and jailed in the effort to smother the movement that became known as "Woman, Life, Freedom."

It's a terrible situation — yet from a distance such horror may simply look like the news cycle ticking around again. Mass media are good at signaling when something big is happening, after all, but less good at conveying the smaller, compelling details that give real life its immediacy.

In "For the Sun After Long Nights," a remarkable work of reportage and personal testimony, Fatemeh Jamalpour and Nilo Tabrizy offer a look at Iran's recent turbulence that is much more affecting and intimate than general readers are likely to get elsewhere. In alternating chapters we hear from Ms. Jamalpour, a journalist who has worked most of her life in Iran (but is officially banned from reporting there), and from Ms. Tabrizy, a reporter for the Washington Post who was a child when her family emigrated to Canada. The narrative roves widely as the authors describe the origins and unfolding of what the book's subtitle calls Iran's "women-led uprising." We learn about the authors' own lives and families, about Iran's modern history and about bold women from earlier generations. Ms. Tabrizy is especially good on the yearnings of exile and on the difficulty of communicating with sources in Iran or verifying the accuracy of claims and videos that make it out of the country. Ms. Jamalpour writes with exceptional eloquence of the caprices, frustrations and peculiarities of life under the clerisy.

We learn from Ms. Jamalpour, for instance, that journalists and activists in Iran keep glasses of water by their beds — not for drinking, but for drowning their cellphones in case the security forces raid at night. We learn that among the normal headaches of wedding planning is the added risk of an attack by local police, if there's any suspicion that the sexes might be partying together. (Ms. Jamalpour remembers one wedding, when she and "dozens of women in high heels and long gowns with hands raised were running to the second floor" to pretend that they were celebrating separately from the men. In that instance, a large bribe settled the matter.)

We learn that girls are required to veil their hair from the age of 7, and that it is a criminal violation to wear boots, makeup, nail polish or sunglasses atop the head. We learn that under Iran's Islamic dress code a woman wearing insufficiently boxy clothing may be denied entry to the airport to catch her flight, as happened to Ms. Jamalpour (and more than 100 other women) in 2007. And throughout the book we see the ingenuity of tech-savvy Iranians working to circumvent the regime's endless attempts to douse their ability to communicate.

Not everything is a downer in this fine book, which for all its grim tidings is suffused with patriotic love. The general reader may be touched to discover, for example, how thoroughly Iranian culture is saturated with poetry. It is the coin of the realm, the great commonality, a meeting point of the heart even between inquisitor and detainee. "Nearly every Iranian, regardless of economic status or educational level, knows the great Persian poets," the authors tell us early on. "Even those who cannot read can recite, from memory, a favorite verse written by Hafez or Rumi."

It is thus fitting that the poetic phrase "Woman, Life, Freedom" appeared in the lyrics of a ballad written after Mahsa Jîna Amini's killing in September 2022. During the ensuing unrest, protestors slipped encouraging handwritten verses into the palms of strangers as they passed. The husband of an imprisoned journalist missed most the voice of his wife, reading poetry at night. And when, on the eve of the book's publication, Ms. Jamalpour's lawyer wanted to give her advice, he did it this way: Unless she wanted to spend the rest of her life like the 11th-century versifier known as the "prisoner poet," she had better flee the country. She did.

  • Mrs. Gurdon, a Journal contributor, is the author of "The Enchanted Hour: The Miraculous Power of Reading Aloud in the Age of Distraction."

data.title: Trump's 100% Drug Tariff Just Landed--Here's Why Wall Street Isn't Panicking
data.description:
President Donald Trump's plan to slap a 100% tariff on branded and patented drug imports may sound seismic, but markets barely flinched. That's because most investors are wagering that the exemption for companies with US-based manufacturing could make the threat more bark than bite. While the policy technically targets imports where plant construction hasn't started by October 1, analysts note that nearly every major drugmaker has either broken ground or ramped up plans to do so. Vontobel's Sibylle Bischofberger Frick put it plainly: Will that prevent their drugs from tariffs? In our view, yes.

Merck , Novo Nordisk , and Eli Lilly are already deep into US buildsfrom Delaware to North Carolina to Texasanchoring supply chains for blockbusters in cancer, diabetes and immunology. Even Novartis and Sanofi have signaled large US investments, though progress remains harder to gauge. Pharma stocks in Europe barely blinked Friday, with GSK even rising slightly. Novo was the rare laggard, falling as much as 3.1%. Jared Holz at Mizuho called the tariff news nebulous to negligible, emphasizing that virtually all big players already have meaningful domestic operations tied to recent capex cycles.

Bloomberg Intelligence estimates about $220 billion worth of pharmaceutical imports could be affected, lifting average tariff rates by 3.3 percentage points. But with Trump offering few specificsand trade deals like the EU's reportedly capping pharma tariffs at 15%the street sees this as more political signaling than imminent disruption. Jefferies analysts framed the move as a net win for the industry, reinforcing its US manufacturing pivot without punishing the sector. For now, investors appear to be treating the tariff threat as another headline with limited impactat least until the details catch up.

data.title: VIP Industries block deal: Promoters sell 6.2% stake for ₹343 crore
data.description:
Promoters of VIP Industries offloaded a 6.2% stake in the company on Thursday through a block deal worth ₹343 crore. Piramal Vibhuti Investments and Kiddy Plast sold 88.4 lakh shares at an average price of ₹388.2 apiece.

Multiples Private Equity Fund picked up 60 lakh shares for ₹233 crore, while Samvibhag Securities acquired 22 lakh shares for ₹86 crore.

The latest transaction comes after a major July deal, when Multiples Private Equity Fund IV, Multiples Private Equity Gift Fund IV, Samvibhag Securities, along with Mithun and Siddhartha Sacheti, together acquired a 32% stake in VIP Industries from a group of promoters.

The consortium had also announced a mandatory open offer for an additional 26% stake in the company. The acquirers will spend an additional ₹1,437 crore to buy up to 3.7 crore shares at ₹388 each under the open offer, taking the overall transaction value for a 58% stake to ₹3,200 crore.

Separately, VIP Industries has seen changes in its leadership. The board approved the resignation of managing director Neetu Kashiramka, effective from the close of its meeting on 23 September.

She will, however, continue to lead the company until October 31, 2025. Atul Jain has been appointed as the new managing director for a five-year term starting 23 September.

Shares of VIP Industries closed 3.7% lower at ₹410.35 on the NSE.

data.title: Mexico's Pemex crude oil exports in August fall 32% year-on-year
data.description:
Mexican state company Pemex exported 500,203 barrels per day (bpd) of crude oil in August, a 32% drop year-on-year, as its local refineries processed slightly more, company data released late on Thursday showed.

Pemex has been trying to squeeze more barrels from several rapidly declining fields, especially those in the Gulf of Mexico, but its operations are constrained by budget constraints and mounting debts.

By next year, exports are forecast to drop to about 487,900 bpd, and over the next decade to about 393,100 bpd.

Pemex's seven local refineries processed just over 1.05 million bpd in August, up from the previous month, the numbers showed.

Crude oil and condensate production in August was 1.64 million bpd, similar to previous months, but lower than the same month last year.

The government of President Claudia Sheinbaum has said it wants to lift crude oil production, which is dominated by Pemex, to reach 1.8 million bpd.

While Mexico is one of the world's largest producers, the country imports refined products because Pemex refineries struggle to efficiently process the heavy sour Maya grade the state company mostly pumps.

Imports of petroleum products, including gasoline and diesel, fell 19% in August compared with the previous month, and 38% year-on-year, to 457,849 bpd.

Pemex's own production of petroleum products that same month was 1.07 million bpd, up 4% year-on-year, but slightly lower than the 1.09 million bpd produced in July.

data.title: GlobalFoundries Stock Climbs. What's Behind the Chip Manufacturer's Momentum. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

GlobalFoundries shares were surging on Friday. The Trump administration's latest plans to boost domestic chip manufacturing could be a boon for the stock.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has discussed a plan with semiconductor executives to potentially oblige chip companies to manufacture the same number of semiconductors in the U.S. as they import from overseas producers, The Wall Street Journal reported Friday, citing people familiar with the concept.

The Department of Commerce didn't immediately respond to a request for comment early on Friday.

GlobalFoundries was up 4.8% in Friday trading. The manufacturer has already committed to a $16 billion investment in U.S. chip manufacturing and could be in line to receive more orders if the administration's plan comes to fruition.

GlobalFoundries was formed through the divestiture of Advanced Micro Devices' manufacturing arm in 2009. AMD subsequently became its first customer, while Qualcomm was another early partner. GlobalFoundries has supplied semiconductors for Apple products, specifically the iPhone, since 2010.

Other U.S. chip manufacturers were also gaining on Friday. Texas Instruments, which said in June that it would invest more than $60 billion in U.S. manufacturing sites, was up 1%. Memory-chip maker Micron Technology was up 0.5%.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

1.   Introduction

This letter is sent to you by Invesco Markets plc (the "Company") which is established as an umbrella fund with segregated liability between sub-funds. Invesco S&P 500 UCITS ETF (the "Fund") is a sub-fund of the Company.

The purpose of this letter is to notify you of an extraordinary general meeting ("EGM") to consider and vote on a proposed stock split of the Acc share class of the Fund (the "Share Class").

2.   Stock split

The Directors, in consultation with the Manager and the Investment Manager, have determined it appropriate to sub-divide the Share Class.

If approved by the Shareholders, the Share Class will be sub-divided by a factor of 100, which is the value determined by the Investment Manager.

The effect of this stock split is twofold. On the Payment Date:

(1)   The net asset value per share of the Share Class will decrease to 1/100th of its value

(2)   For each share of the Share Class held, a Shareholder will receive, free of payment, an additional 99 shares, resulting in an increase in their total number of shares held by the same factor of 100

By simultaneously decreasing the net asset value per share by a factor of 100, and increasing the number of shares by same factor of 100 (the "Stock Split"), there will be no change in (a) the total net asset value of all outstanding shares in the Share Class, (b) the total net asset value of the Fund, or (c) the value of any Shareholder's holding in the Fund.

The Directors have determined that the Stock Split is appropriate on the basis that the reduced net asset value per share of the Share Class will facilitate accessibility for prospective investors, thereby facilitating further investment in the Fund and increasing Fund assets to the benefit of all investors. The reduced net asset value per share will also allow both new and existing investors to increase or decrease the size of their investment more precisely.

If approved by Shareholders, the Stock Split is due to take effect on or around 15 December 2025 or such other date as notified to Shareholders (the "Payment Date").

3.   Shareholders should note that:

·      Shareholders will not bear any additional legal or administrative costs as a result of the Stock Split.

·      There will be no change to any of the identifiers of the Share Class (e.g., ISIN, exchange codes).

·      There will be no impact on the Fund (other than an increase in the number of shares of the Share Class) or any of its other Share Classes.

·      The Stock Split will have no impact on the value of your investment.

The Directors believe that the resolution to be proposed at the EGM is in the best interests of Shareholders and, accordingly, the Directors recommend that Shareholders vote in favour of the resolution.

NOTICE OF EGM TO CONSIDER AND VOTE ON STOCK SPLIT

In order to obtain Shareholder approval for the Stock Split, the Directors have determined to convene an EGM at which a resolution to approve the Stock Split will be proposed.

You will find attached to this letter a notice of the EGM ("Notice of EGM") which will be held at 10 a.m. (Irish time) on 23 October 2025.

For a Shareholder's vote to be counted at the EGM, the form must be received by Citibank Europe plc, the Common Depositary no later than 48 hours before the time fixed for the holding of the EGM.

Publication of Results

Shareholders who do not wish to remain in the Share Class following the implementation of the Stock Split (if the resolution is passed) will have the opportunity to redeem their Shares on any Dealing Day prior to the Payment Date in the manner prescribed in the Prospectus.

data.title: AppLovin Stock Keeps on Climbing. Why It Still Has Room to Rise. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

AppLovin stock has been on a tear over the last year, and the mobile technology company isn't slowing down any time soon, analysts say.

The company, which provides monetization tools to mobile app developers, scored two price-target boosts from Wall Street on Thursday ahead of the soft launch of its self-serve Axon Ads Manager platform next month.

AppLovin shares were up 2.8% to $658.08 on Friday. The stock has climbed 103% this year and more than 400% over the last 12 months, helping to earn it a place in the S&P 500.

Piper Sandler reiterated an Overweight rating and raised its price target on the stock to $740, touting the potential of Axon Ads Manager, which is powered by the company's new Axon 2.0 artificial-intelligence engine.

"Management is signaling confidence on the product, which we think is warranted," Piper Sandler's James Callahan wrote. "While we expect the rollout to be challenging and non-linear, excitement seems appropriate."

Axon adds to other AI-forward products like Dynamic Product Ads, which use AI to scrape sites to generate imagery for ads. Other than Meta Platforms, most social media names are still trying to build out this kind of tool, Callahan said. The result? Better outcomes and more spending from major advertisers.

Analysts at UBS also see a positive response to AppLovin's offerings among both e-commerce and gaming advertisers. The firm is confident about AppLovin's demand even though some industry experts see growth moderating either now or in the coming quarters.

"We're reiterating APP as our top pick," wrote Chris Kuntarich of UBS. "Over the next 12-months, we expect APP to execute on a combination of demand and supply expansion initiatives."

The firm maintained a Buy rating and boosted its price target on the stock to $810 from $540.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

data.title: This is flying under the radar but could yield risk-off moves in popular tech stocks, says Bank of America
data.description:
By Barbara Kollmeyer

Watch for other riskier areas of the stock market to pull back, say B. of A. strategists

Investors may not be thinking about stress on the popular tech trade coming from riskier areas of the market, warns Bank of America warns.

While some investors may be voicing concerns about the money chasing AI, when it comes to the sums being lent to those big companies there seems little panic.

That's according to Bank of America strategists led by Michael Hartnett, who say that the "most important new metric of risk" surrounding the boom in artificial-intelligence data centers are tech-credit spreads. Those "well-behaved" spreads are presently at 18-year lows, indicating investors are not demanding yield compensation to fund that data buildout, they note.

Need to Know: Are markets getting wary on AI spending? Here's what billionaire hedge fund founder David Einhorn thinks.

Here's their chart putting it into perspective:

Here's another from Bank of America showing the rising level of U.S. private data-center spending, versus falling office spending:

The strategists, however, warned clients in a note on Friday that they are watching potential trouble from other corners of the market. They cited three potential red flags, starting with the dollar.

A "disorderly unwind" of the popular short-dollar trade, whereas investors bet on the greenback to keep falling, would see investors rush to close those bearish bets. The DXY Dollar Index DXY has fallen 9% in 2025, weighed upon by expectations for interest-rate cuts as well as concerns over the independence of the Federal Reserve.

Opinion: 'People have predicted dollar doom for decades. What makes this time actually different?'

The second is a potential reversal in emerging-markets currencies - they noted that the MSCI Emerging Markets Currency Index has broken out of a long-term technical level of 1,700 and are monitoring to see if that holds. The WisdomTree Emerging Currency Strategy Fund CEW, for example, has been on a steady rise this year. Part of the appetite for those currencies has come from expectations for looser Fed policy.

The third red flag would be widening spreads on risky high-yield corporate bonds, as a rise in those spreads would mean investors are demanding a higher premium to hold that debt.

In short, if the above assets start to reverse direction and investors pull out of them, that momentum could feed into jitters for the most popular trades, such as bets on tech stocks COMP and rising long-term rates, warn Hartnett and his team.

Note, while equity investors in AI could theoretically lose it all if their shares plunge in value, debt investors in - for example - Oracle (ORCL) bonds still have protection in a scenario where a big U.S. company faces financial challenges.

Read on: Why Oracle's 'jumbo' AI-fueled bond deal is so unusual

-Barbara Kollmeyer

data.title: Fiction: Emmelie Prophète's 'Cécé'
data.description:
By Sam Sacks

The narrator of Emmelie Prophète's "Cécé" (Archipelago, 224 pages, $18) is Célia Jérôme, a destitute young woman in Haiti's gang-ridden Cité of Divine Power, a "lost corner of a lost country, a boil on the lip of a sick nation." Célia lives with her alcoholic uncle and scrapes together money by "entertaining" male visitors, though she hates the work and doesn't have many clients. Her prospects are sparse, her hopes sparser. But online she is Cécé la Flamme, a freewheeling Facebook personality with tens of thousands of followers. "I mostly posted pictures of my feet, food I bought at Morel's," referring to a local restaurant, "the gullies in the Cité, dead bodies I came across on the street," she explains. "Dead bodies did very well."

Ms. Prophète's memorably macabre novel depicts Célia's double life of hiding from gunfire in the streets while racking up likes on her phone. Haiti emerges as a country broken beyond repair. Célia's cité, or slum, is controlled by an upstart gang whose rules of succession are simple and stupid: Whoever kills the boss becomes the boss. So every few months leadership violently changes hands, claimed by another crazed, desperate young man "aware of his power and his own looming death."

Célia knows a lot of these guys from the neighborhood and her online popularity means that she can't escape their notice. The gang bosses, who all have their own social-media accounts, demand posts praising their rule. But mostly the internet is Célia's sanctuary, a place of freedom and possibility — a dream world where she can "exist more than ever before."

Ms. Prophète's achievement is to make the real and the virtual sides of Célia's life equally vivid. This is a voice-driven novel, and Aidan Rooney's terrific translation from the French conveys the heroine's profound despair and snarky resilience. Célia is opinionated, vulnerable, mordantly funny and often overwhelmed. Late in the book she is hired as an influencer and begins hawking dubious beauty products. At these moments she could be any wised-up Zoomer turning the attention economy to her advantage, acting for a throng of followers whose "existence hinged on whether my phone's battery was charged or not." She's more than a hard-nosed cynic, however. Status updates are her expressions of defiance and determination. For Célia, to post is to be.

The pensive voice narrating Claire Adam's "Love Forms" (Hogarth, 288 pages, $28) belongs to Dawn Bishop, a 58-year-old London divorcée who recounts her childhood in Trinidad with family wealth from the tinned-fruit business. When Dawn was 16 she became pregnant after a reckless night with a stranger during the island's Carnival celebrations. Frightened of a scandal, her parents smuggled her to a convent in Venezuela, where she gave birth and left the baby for adoption. Ever since, the Bishops have upheld a "pact" of silence about the incident, but Dawn, despite moving abroad, marrying and having two other children, has remained haunted by the daughter she gave up.

When the novel starts, a woman with an adoption history that roughly fits the timeline of the event has responded to a long-shot search query that Dawn placed online. Dawn has been disappointed by potential matches before, and as the story builds toward the women's decision to take a DNA test, it sensitively fleshes out Dawn's daily life, her brittle relationship with her family and her groping yet richly described recollections of growing up in the "dark, fuming, forest green of the tropics."

Patience is the virtue of this quiet, naturalistic novel, which slowly expands around absences. Dawn's missing daughter is a symbol of a life unclaimed. In London, Dawn trained to be a doctor but abandoned her career to raise her sons. Though Ms. Adam is a different sort of stylist, she produces echoes of Jean Rhys, whose disaffected London heroines cast back nostalgically to their Caribbean childhoods. In place of Rhys's hovels and dive bars, Ms. Adam evokes the sterile English suburbs. Trinidad persists for Dawn as a site of original sin and a lost idyll. With little ado, "Love Forms" culminates in a family reunion on the island, staging reckonings that touchingly explore the ache and joy of memory.

In Kiran Desai's big, warmly written dual-family saga, "The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny" (Hogarth, 688 pages, $32), an eminent elder remarks, "exile and love both undo your sense of self." If this is true, then Sonia Shah and Sunny Bhatia are doubly undone. Sonia has left her large family in Delhi to study literature in New England, where she falls into a relationship with a vain and manipulative painter. Sunny has moved from India to New York to work as a journalist, and he has an American girlfriend whom he is too scared to introduce to his domineering mother. Both feel stifled in India but isolated in the U.S. They can't figure out who they are or where they should be.

It happens that back in India, Sonia's and Sunny's grandfathers are acquaintances. Distressed by Sonia's melancholy, the Shahs secretly inquire with the Bhatias about an arranged marriage. Sunny dismisses these machinations — so old-fashioned! — but later, after their respective relationships have fallen apart, he and Sonia meet and feel an instantaneous spark. Ms. Desai shrewdly portions out the scenes in which Sonia and Sunny are together, sustaining a feeling of low-boil suspense about their fate as a couple throughout the book, which moves from the 1990s into the 2000s and indulges in countless colorful digressions. "This was India," Sonia thinks, "you might try to write a slender story, but it inevitably connected to a larger one." The novel's length also owes to its ambivalence. As the characters feel split between countries and cultures, so does the book fluctuate between the traditional and the modern. Is this the chronicle of an arranged relationship or a passionate love affair? Ms. Desai's smart, sprawling work seems to ask: Why not both?

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1138 ET - A joint task force partnering the USDA and DOJ to investigate the high costs for farmer inputs like fertilizer and seeds is getting praise from groups like the National Farmers Union. "When just a handful of companies control the markets for seed, fertilizer and other farm supplies, they can raise prices at will," says the NFU in a note. "This leaves farmers squeezed between skyrocketing costs and low prices for their products." CBOT grain futures are mostly below the levels needed for farmers to turn a profit on their acres, and these farmers need both improved crop prices and lower input costs in order for these acres to turn positive. CBOT grains are mixed, with corn down 0.8%, soybeans up 0.1%, and wheat down 1.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1051 ET - U.S. financial support should allow Argentina's President Milei to secure a political victory in midterm elections a month from now, Payden & Rygel's Alexis Roach writes. This outcome would likely ease investors' fears of a deviation from fiscal austerity as the country fights crippling inflation and currency weakness. Following the election, the task will be to amass support from lawmakers. "Just as importantly, Argentina should position itself to regain market access," Roach says. She also calls for a policy shift allowing the central bank to accumulate international reserves aggressively. "This should translate into changing the monetary framework to allow for more currency flexibility." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0851 ET - Truck customers are likely to wait and see how new U.S. tariffs influence pricing, so expect orders and demand to take a hit in the near term, Jefferies analysts write. If prices increase across the industry in response, demand will likely be ht in the fourth quarter and into 2026, they add. Trump plans to impose 25% tariffs on heavy-duty trucks, but it isn't known how vehicles with parts made in the U.S., or which are USMCA-compliant, will be treated. Jefferies estimates that Daimler Truck and Traton could be at a 300-600 basis point cost disadvantage to manufacturers that assemble most vehicles in the U.S., such as Volvo and Paccar. Daimler Truck shares fall 2.3%, Traton shares are down 2% while Volvo shares rise 3.4%. Paccar shares rise 6.7% pre-market. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0840 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs came with an exception for companies building new factories in the U.S., but short-term levies could still be faced if projects aren't under way by Oct. 1, Panmure Liberum analysts write. Britain's GSK and AstraZeneca have announced major investments in the U.S., which might shield them, they write. However, those programs won't launch until 2026, a deadline that may expose the groups to short-term levies, the analysts say. Meanwhile, the announcement was encouraging for phrama groups that focus on generic drugs, as they appear to be exempt from tariff plans, they add. Hikma and Sandoz shares are up 1.7% and 1.3%, respectively, while GSK and AstraZeneca are steady. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0813 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs shouldn't affect Belgian pharma group UCB as the company outlined plans to build a manufacturing site in the country, ING analysts write. ING says that, as far as it is aware, the 15% ceiling on U.S. levies on imports from the EU applies to branded pharmaceuticals under the recent U.S.-EU tariff deal, the analysts say. Moreover, 65% of UCB's products are manufactured by third parties worldwide, they add. UCB said in June that it would build a manufacturing plant in the U.S. that would create 300 permanent and 500 construction jobs. UCB shares rise 0.8% and are up 3.1% year-on-year. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0757 ET - It is unclear how new U.S. drug tariffs relate to a U.S.-EU trade deal capping levies, Novo Nordisk CEO Mike Doustdar says. The Danish pharma group looks forward to working with the Trump administration, but it is uncertain whether the earlier deal between the U.S. and the EU will keep tariffs at 15% rather than the 100% announced by President Trump, Doustdar writes in a statement. Construction is under way on a Novo Nordisk manufacturing site in North Carolina, he adds. Trump announced Thursday that the tariffs would hit pharmaceutical companies not building U.S. plants. Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.7%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0405 ET - Oil prices are on track for a significant weekly gain following news that Russia plans to temporarily ban diesel exports from resellers and extend its gasoline-export ban amid Ukrainian drone strikes against refineries. In early trade, Brent crude ticks 0.1% higher to $68.66 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.2% to $65.13 a barrel. The benchmarks continue to trade in a narrow range, but are up 3% and 4.4% on the week, respectively. Geopolitical tensions are high, with European diplomats warning the Kremlin that NATO is prepared to take action against further violations of its airspace, including by shooting down Russian planes, according to a Bloomberg report. However, further gains are capped by uncertainties over the macroeconomic outlook, with President Trump saying he would slap new tariffs on pharma and large trucks, and a looming global supply surplus.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0405 ET - President Trump's drug tariffs will have a limited impact on big Swiss pharma players given their U.S. presence, Vontobel analysts write. While there is broad room for interpretation of Trump's announcement, Roche and Novartis are shielded by their U.S. footprints, while Sandoz should be unaffected since it focuses on exempted generic products, the analysts say. Galderma's exposure is less clear as it has a smaller U.S. presence. Tariffs will increase U.S. drug prices and supply concerns, but they shouldn't threaten the country's leading position in drug development, Vontobel says. Galderma shares fall 3.2%, with Roche and Novartis down 0.3% and 0.2%, respectively. Sandoz shares trade broadly flat. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0337 ET - European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

0318 ET - President Trump's announcement of 25% tariffs on imported heavy trucks is light on detail, but Daimler Truck could suffer the most given current production in the U.S. and overall exposure to the U.S. at the group level, Bernstein analysts write. U.S. manufacturer Paccar could be the greatest beneficiary, it adds. Bernstein notes the tariffs apply from Oct. 1, so they won't impact the third quarter, where orders and volumes are weak for all manufacturers. It adds that further inflationary increases to truck prices will negatively impact all, especially with new truck prices currently considerably elevated after many years of price increases. It rates Volvo and Daimler Truck at underperform and Traton at market-perform. Volvo shares rise 2.1%, Daimler Truck shares fall 4.4% and Traton shares drop 2.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0301 ET - The dollar turns lower after President Trump announced a new round of tariffs, including a 100% tariff on drugs by companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. as well as levies on large trucks and home goods, beginning Oct. 1. The dollar had jumped to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. economic data, including a sizeable upgrade to U.S. second-quarter GDP. This damped prospects of interest-rate cuts. However, Commerzbank analyst Michael Pfister says the U.S. economy's dependence on private consumption poses a risk if sentiment declines. Trump's "erratic trade policy is doing much to dampen consumer sentiment," he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.409 after hitting a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0300 ET - The market likely doesn't believe the U.S.'s pharmaceutical tariffs will have real, significant bite in the end, says Citi Wealth's Ken Peng in a media roundtable. The head of Asia investment strategy notes President Trump offered an "escape" for pharma manufacturers, as they will be exempted from the tariffs as long as they start to build a plant in the U.S. While Asian pharma names fell after the news, Peng says: "If people really believed that large swaths of branded pharma would be tariffed at 100%, [the shares' movement] wouldn't be a 3%-4% correction... I think the market is calling it bluff." The new tariffs are also unlikely to hurt U.S.-China trade negotiations much, which he views as more important. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)
